IMPACT.justification: [Consensus avg=4] A(4): The paper addresses an important bottleneck for data-to-text micro-planning by proposing a semi-automatic framework that generates corpora from KBs (DBpedia) and by releasing DBPNLG, which the authors show is more semantically and linguistically diverse than the widely used RNNLG dataset. The finding that standard multilayer seq2seq models obtain higher perplexity and lower BLEU on DBPNLG highlights a gap in current modeling and is likely to change how researchers evaluate and develop NLG micro-planners. Practical limitations (coverage currently reported for only 6 DBpedia categories, manual property clarification, and an 8.7% corpus loss) reduce immediate broad adoption but do not negate the dataset’s and framework’s potential to open significant new research directions in KB verbalisation and micro-planning. | B(4): The paper targets an important problem—creating diverse, KB-derived training corpora for NLG micro-planning—and provides a semi-automatic framework that yields DBPNLG, which the authors show is more semantically and linguistically diverse than the crowdsourced RNNLG and more challenging for standard seq2seq models (higher perplexity, lower BLEU). By demonstrating that KB-extracted corpora can better exercise micro-planning subtasks (lexicalisation, aggregation, sentence segmentation, referring expression generation) and be used for KB verbalizers, the work is likely to change how researchers construct training data and to open new research directions. Impact is tempered by reported limitations (dataset currently reported for only 6 DBpedia categories, manual property clarification, and an 8.7% corpus loss), so the contribution is strong but not fully transformative.

By demonstrating that KB-extracted corpora can better exercise micro-planning subtasks (lexicalisation, aggregation, sentence segmentation, referring expression generation) and be used for KB verbalizers, the work is likely to change how researchers construct training data and to open new research directions. APPROPRIATENESS.justification: [Consensus avg=5] A(5): The paper fits squarely within top NLP/ML venues: it addresses data-to-text NLG micro-planning and introduces DBPNLG derived from DBpedia, a resource and task of clear relevance to the NLG community. The methods (KB graph extraction, ILP subtree selection, staged crowdsourcing) and evaluations against RNNLG and a seq2seq baseline using standard metrics (BLEU, perplexity, lexical sophistication, CTTR) meet the methodological and evaluation expectations of such conferences. It is aimed at the right audience (NLG/ML researchers), is positioned with thorough related work coverage, and presents a dataset and empirical findings that would interest the venue's community. | B(5): This paper fits squarely within top-tier NLP/ML venues: it addresses data-to-text NLG and micro-planning, constructs a novel resource (DBPNLG) from DBpedia, and evaluates it against established benchmarks (RNNLG) and baselines (multilayer seq2seq with attention) using standard metrics (BLEU, perplexity, lexical measures). The methodology (KB subtree selection, crowdsourced verbalizations, ILP selection, property bigram models) and the focus on creating a dataset to drive research on lexicalisation, aggregation, sentence segmentation, and referring expressions target the venue's audience and research community. Although the current release covers only six categories and required some manual property clarification, the paper uses methods and evaluation standards expected at the venue and is positioned appropriately for NLP/ML researchers.

The methods (KB graph extraction, ILP subtree selection, staged crowdsourcing) and evaluations against RNNLG and a seq2seq baseline using standard metrics (BLEU, perplexity, lexical sophistication, CTTR) meet the methodological and evaluation expectations of such conferences. It is aimed at the right audience (NLG/ML researchers), is positioned with thorough related work coverage, and presents a dataset and empirical findings that would interest the venue's community.

reviewer_b.justification: This paper fits squarely within top-tier NLP/ML venues: it addresses data-to-text NLG and micro-planning, constructs a novel resource (DBPNLG) from DBpedia, and evaluates it against established benchmarks (RNNLG) and baselines (multilayer seq2seq with attention) using standard metrics (BLEU, perplexity, lexical measures). The methodology (KB subtree selection, crowdsourced verbalizations, ILP selection, property bigram models) and the focus on creating a dataset to drive research on lexicalisation, aggregation, sentence segmentation, and referring expressions target the venue's audience and research community. Although the current release covers only six categories and required some manual property clarification, the paper uses methods and evaluation standards expected at the venue and is positioned appropriately for NLP/ML researchers.

ORIGINALITY.justification: [Consensus avg=4] A(4): The paper presents a notable and practically useful new pipeline — a semi-automatic framework that turns KB graphs (DBpedia) into corpora for NLG micro-planning, combining property-bigram models (S/C/M models), ILP subtree selection and a three-stage crowdsourcing verification — which is a clear departure from prior crowdsourced datasets like RNNLG. The authors explicitly differentiate their DBPNLG dataset (more semantic/linguistic diversity and greater difficulty for seq2seq models) from existing work and report concrete empirical evidence of increased challenge. While many components are established (KB extraction, ILP selection, crowdsourcing), their composition and targeted focus on micro-planning corpus creation is a non-trivial, informatively new contribution that would interest experts in data-to-text generation. | B(4): The paper presents a notable new perspective by proposing a semi-automatic pipeline to mine DBpedia and produce micro-planning corpora (DBPNLG), contrasting with purely crowdsourced resources like RNNLG. Its combination of learned property-bigram models (S-/C-/M-Models), ILP subtree selection for data units, and a three-stage crowdsourcing/verification protocol is a non-trivial methodological assembly that is clearly differentiated from prior work. While elements (KB extraction, ILP, crowdsourcing) are existing techniques, their integration specifically to support wide-coverage NLG micro-planning and the demonstrated increase in linguistic/semantic complexity constitute meaningful novelty, despite limitations such as coverage restricted to 6 categories and some manual property clarification.

reviewer_a.justification: The paper presents a notable and practically useful new pipeline — a semi-automatic framework that turns KB graphs (DBpedia) into corpora for NLG micro-planning, combining property-bigram models (S/C/M models), ILP subtree selection and a three-stage crowdsourcing verification — which is a clear departure from prior crowdsourced datasets like RNNLG. The authors explicitly differentiate their DBPNLG dataset (more semantic/linguistic diversity and greater difficulty for seq2seq models) from existing work and report concrete empirical evidence of increased challenge. While many components are established (KB extraction, ILP selection, crowdsourcing), their composition and targeted focus on micro-planning corpus creation is a non-trivial, informatively new contribution that would interest experts in data-to-text generation.

reviewer_b.justification: The paper presents a notable new perspective by proposing a semi-automatic pipeline to mine DBpedia and produce micro-planning corpora (DBPNLG), contrasting with purely crowdsourced resources like RNNLG. Its combination of learned property-bigram models (S-/C-/M-Models), ILP subtree selection for data units, and a three-stage crowdsourcing/verification protocol is a non-trivial methodological assembly that is clearly differentiated from prior work. While elements (KB extraction, ILP, crowdsourcing) are existing techniques, their integration specifically to support wide-coverage NLG micro-planning and the demonstrated increase in linguistic/semantic complexity constitute meaningful novelty, despite limitations such as coverage restricted to 6 categories and some manual property clarification.

MEANINGFUL_COMPARISON.justification: [Consensus avg=4] A(4): The paper compares its new DBPNLG corpus to the relevant crowdsourced RNNLG dataset (Wen et al., 2016) and evaluates a standard multilayer sequence-to-sequence with attention (Vinyals et al., 2015) on both datasets using the same metrics (BLEU, perplexity), which makes the comparisons fair. It also reports that standard seq2seq performs worse on the richer DBPNLG and discusses why the new corpus is more challenging, and the related-work section is described as thorough. However, comparisons are somewhat limited in scope (only a single neural baseline is reported and more recent or specialized micro-planning/generative baselines are not empirically evaluated), so the evaluation has minor omissions. | B(4): The paper compares its new DBPNLG resource against a relevant prior corpus (RNNLG, Wen et al. 2016) and evaluates a standard neural baseline (multilayer seq2seq with attention) using comparable metrics (BLEU, perplexity) and reported hyperparameters, and related work coverage is described as thorough. Comparisons appear fair (same evaluation metrics, models evaluated on both corpora) and the paper analyzes why DBPNLG is more challenging (lexical/structural diversity metrics, CTTR, LS, etc.). However, only a single standard seq2seq baseline is reported (Vinyals et al., 2015), so more recent or varied neural baselines/architectures are missing, a minor omission that prevents a fully comprehensive comparison.

reviewer_a.justification: The paper compares its new DBPNLG corpus to the relevant crowdsourced RNNLG dataset (Wen et al., 2016) and evaluates a standard multilayer sequence-to-sequence with attention (Vinyals et al., 2015) on both datasets using the same metrics (BLEU, perplexity), which makes the comparisons fair. It also reports that standard seq2seq performs worse on the richer DBPNLG and discusses why the new corpus is more challenging, and the related-work section is described as thorough. However, comparisons are somewhat limited in scope (only a single neural baseline is reported and more recent or specialized micro-planning/generative baselines are not empirically evaluated), so the evaluation has minor omissions.

reviewer_b.justification: The paper compares its new DBPNLG resource against a relevant prior corpus (RNNLG, Wen et al. 2016) and evaluates a standard neural baseline (multilayer seq2seq with attention) using comparable metrics (BLEU, perplexity) and reported hyperparameters, and related work coverage is described as thorough. Comparisons appear fair (same evaluation metrics, models evaluated on both corpora) and the paper analyzes why DBPNLG is more challenging (lexical/structural diversity metrics, CTTR, LS, etc.). However, only a single standard seq2seq baseline is reported (Vinyals et al., 2015), so more recent or varied neural baselines/architectures are missing, a minor omission that prevents a fully comprehensive comparison.

SUBSTANCE.justification: [Consensus avg=4] A(4): The paper provides solid empirical evidence for its claims: it creates DBPNLG from DBpedia, compares it against RNNLG on multiple linguistic and semantic diversity metrics, and evaluates a standard multilayer seq2seq baseline showing higher perplexity and lower BLEU on DBPNLG. Related work appears thoroughly covered and several limitations are explicitly discussed (limited category coverage, manual property clarification, 8.7% corpus loss, and neural models not being optimal). However, the summary does not indicate thorough ablation or sensitivity analyses of the semi-automatic framework components, and reproducibility is only partial (hyperparameters reported but code not mentioned and some manual preprocessing required), so the experimental analysis has minor gaps. | B(4): The paper provides solid empirical evidence: creation of DBPNLG with multiple corpus-level and input-shape metrics, a direct comparison to RNNLG, and evaluation of a seq2seq baseline showing higher perplexity and lower BLEU on the new data. Related work coverage is reported as thorough and several limitations are acknowledged (only 6 DBpedia categories released, manual property clarification, 8.7% corpus loss). However, the summary indicates no explicit ablation or sensitivity analyses of design choices (e.g., the S/C/M property models or ILP selection), and reproducibility is partially limited (code not mentioned, some manual steps), so a minor gap prevents an excellent rating.

reviewer_a.justification: The paper provides solid empirical evidence for its claims: it creates DBPNLG from DBpedia, compares it against RNNLG on multiple linguistic and semantic diversity metrics, and evaluates a standard multilayer seq2seq baseline showing higher perplexity and lower BLEU on DBPNLG. Related work appears thoroughly covered and several limitations are explicitly discussed (limited category coverage, manual property clarification, 8.7% corpus loss, and neural models not being optimal). However, the summary does not indicate thorough ablation or sensitivity analyses of the semi-automatic framework components, and reproducibility is only partial (hyperparameters reported but code not mentioned and some manual preprocessing required), so the experimental analysis has minor gaps.

reviewer_b.justification: The paper provides solid empirical evidence: creation of DBPNLG with multiple corpus-level and input-shape metrics, a direct comparison to RNNLG, and evaluation of a seq2seq baseline showing higher perplexity and lower BLEU on the new data. Related work coverage is reported as thorough and several limitations are acknowledged (only 6 DBpedia categories released, manual property clarification, 8.7% corpus loss). However, the summary indicates no explicit ablation or sensitivity analyses of design choices (e.g., the S/C/M property models or ILP selection), and reproducibility is partially limited (code not mentioned, some manual steps), so a minor gap prevents an excellent rating.

CLARITY.justification: [Consensus avg=4] A(4): The paper is well organized and follows a clear flow from problem statement through methodology (graph extraction, property bigram models, ILP selection, three-stage crowdsourcing) to experiments and conclusions. The main contributions are stated early, and the writing quality is described as good; notation for the core models (S-/C-/M-Models) and the experimental metrics is consistently introduced. Figures/tables/examples appear supported by a wide range of evaluation metrics, though some presentation choices could be tightened (e.g., clearer discussion of the limited current coverage of only 6 DBpedia categories and the manual property clarifications). | B(4): The paper is well organized with a clear methodological progression (KB extraction -> modelling -> ILP selection -> staged crowdsourcing) and experiments that follow naturally to the conclusion. The main contributions are stated early and the notation for the property bigram models (S-, C-, M-Models) is consistently used. The many quantitative tables/metrics (e.g. attribute counts, CTTR, perplexity, BLEU) and examples make results easy to interpret, and overall writing quality is good. Minor presentation issues remain (e.g. the limited reported coverage of DBPNLG and the fact that several DBpedia properties required manual clarification could be highlighted or discussed more prominently).

SOUNDNESS_CORRECTNESS.justification: [Consensus avg=4] A(4): The paper’s main claims are generally supported by empirical evidence: the authors construct DBPNLG from DBpedia and report a range of corpus-level measures (attributes, input patterns, CTTR, LS) and generation metrics (perplexity, BLEU) showing DBPNLG is more diverse and more challenging for a multilayer seq2seq baseline than RNNLG. There are no apparent mathematical errors (no complex derivations required) and experimental procedures are described (crowdsourcing pipeline, ILP selection, hyperparameters). However, there are notable methodological limitations that weaken the strength of universal claims: the released corpus covers only 6 DBpedia categories (limiting claims of wide coverage), some DBpedia property lexicalisations were manually clarified (potentially biasing the data), and no mention of statistical significance or additional generation baselines is given. Overall the technical work is sound but these issues justify a minor-revision rating rather than perfect score. | B(4): The main claims are generally supported: the paper describes a coherent semi-automatic pipeline (graph extraction, property models, ILP selection, multi-stage crowdsourcing) and provides empirical evidence (diversity metrics like CTTR/LS, input shape counts, tokens/sentences, and higher perplexity/lower BLEU for seq2seq) that DBPNLG is more diverse and more challenging than RNNLG. Mathematical aspects appear limited and the described models/ILP are plausible; hyperparameters and evaluation metrics are reported. However, there are minor technical concerns: the released DBPNLG currently covers only 6 DBpedia categories and required manual clarification of properties (limiting claims of full automation), and the experiments rely on a single seq2seq baseline with no reported statistical significance testing. These issues do not invalidate the main conclusions but warrant caution about generality and robustness.

reviewer_a.justification: The paper’s main claims are generally supported by empirical evidence: the authors construct DBPNLG from DBpedia and report a range of corpus-level measures (attributes, input patterns, CTTR, LS) and generation metrics (perplexity, BLEU) showing DBPNLG is more diverse and more challenging for a multilayer seq2seq baseline than RNNLG. There are no apparent mathematical errors (no complex derivations required) and experimental procedures are described (crowdsourcing pipeline, ILP selection, hyperparameters). However, there are notable methodological limitations that weaken the strength of universal claims: the released corpus covers only 6 DBpedia categories (limiting claims of wide coverage), some DBpedia property lexicalisations were manually clarified (potentially biasing the data), and no mention of statistical significance or additional generation baselines is given. Overall the technical work is sound but these issues justify a minor-revision rating rather than perfect score.

reviewer_b.justification: The main claims are generally supported: the paper describes a coherent semi-automatic pipeline (graph extraction, property models, ILP selection, multi-stage crowdsourcing) and provides empirical evidence (diversity metrics like CTTR/LS, input shape counts, tokens/sentences, and higher perplexity/lower BLEU for seq2seq) that DBPNLG is more diverse and more challenging than RNNLG. Mathematical aspects appear limited and the described models/ILP are plausible; hyperparameters and evaluation metrics are reported. However, there are minor technical concerns: the released DBPNLG currently covers only 6 DBpedia categories and required manual clarification of properties (limiting claims of full automation), and the experiments rely on a single seq2seq baseline with no reported statistical significance testing. These issues do not invalidate the main conclusions but warrant caution about generality and robustness.

main_claims: A semi-automatic framework can create data-to-text corpora from existing knowledge bases (e.g., DBpedia) that support learning of wide-coverage NLG micro-planners.

Applying the framework to DBpedia yields a dataset (DBPNLG) that is more semantically and linguistically diverse than the crowdsourced RNNLG dataset of Wen et al. (2016), despite being smaller in size.
The DBPNLG dataset is more challenging for current neural sequence-to-sequence models: a multilayer seq2seq with attention attains higher perplexity and lower BLEU on DBPNLG than on RNNLG.

The framework produces datasets appropriate for training KB verbalisers and for exercising micro-planning subtasks (lexicalisation, aggregation, sentence segmentation, referring expression generation).
_summary.methodology: Extract category-specific entity graphs from a KB (DBpedia), learn property bigram models (S-Model, C-Model, M-Model) from these graphs, select subtrees via an ILP for data units (1–7 triples), then crowdsource verbalizations in three stages (single-triple sentences, merged multi-triple texts, and crowdsourced verification with majority-vote validation).
baselines: multilayer sequence-to-sequence with attention (Vinyals et al., 2015)
limitations: Current reported DBPNLG in the paper covers only 6 DBpedia categories (authors state they have collected for 14 further categories but texts not yet collected).
Manual clarification of DBpedia properties was required (properties with unclear lexicalisations were manually clarified).
Corpus verification resulted in a total corpus loss of 8.7%.
Experiments indicate standard seq2seq models are not optimal for generating linguistically complex texts from the richer DBPNLG dataset.
